query: We're looking at leaning on the bigger hulls for Chumphon instead of the little ships. Suppose we require that the men lifted out of Chumphon on small ships never exceed the men lifted out of Chumphon on the large ships. Plug that in and tell me where total man-miles land.
expected_pyomo: model.chum_balance = Constraint(expr=sum(model.y[vv, kk, qq] for (vv, kk, qq) in model.vkp if qq == 'chumphon' and kk == 'small') <= sum(model.y[vv, kk, qq] for (vv, kk, qq) in model.vkp if qq == 'chumphon' and kk == 'large'))

query: Engineering is pitching a minimum medium-ship footprint at every port we serve with medium hulls, to keep those crews current. The idea: at each such port, the medium ships should lift at least 100 of its men. What does that do to the total man-miles?
expected_pyomo:
def _r(model, pp):
    idx = [(v2, k2, qq) for (v2, k2, qq) in model.vkp if qq == pp and k2 == 'medium']
    return sum(model.y[v2, k2, qq] for (v2, k2, qq) in idx) >= 100 if idx else Constraint.Skip
model.med_floor = Constraint(model.p, rule=_r)

query: Chumphon's men almost all ride the cheap near-shore runs while the long-haul large voyages skip it. Shouldn't the plan at least pull a fair share of Chumphon through the deep-water voyages — say put a combined 300 of its men across the far large-ship runs v-07, v-12, v-13 and v-15? What's the model seeing that keeps it from spreading the load that way?
expected_pyomo: model.chum_distant = Constraint(expr=sum(model.y[vv, 'large', qq] for (vv, kk, qq) in model.vkp if qq == 'chumphon' and kk == 'large' and vv in ('v-07', 'v-12', 'v-13', 'v-15')) >= 300)

query: Loading 1123 men onto the shared voyage v-09 feels like all our eggs in one basket while v-07 and v-10 — both of which can reach Songkhla — barely touch it. Shouldn't what v-09 carries into Songkhla be held no higher than what those two runs carry into Songkhla combined? What's pulling the optimizer toward concentrating it on v-09?

query: Some ports barely see a large ship at all, which leaves us exposed if the smaller hulls go down for maintenance. Wouldn't it be more robust if every port had its large ships carrying at least half of its own headcount? What's the trade-off the model is making by leaning so unevenly on the small and medium boats?
expected_pyomo:
def _r(model, pp):
    idx = [(v2, k2, qq) for (v2, k2, qq) in model.vkp if qq == pp and k2 == 'large']
    return sum(model.y[v2, k2, qq] for (v2, k2, qq) in idx) >= 0.5 * value(model.d[pp]) if idx else Constraint.Skip
model.large_floor = Constraint(model.p, rule=_r)

query: The schedule rides hard on the shared voyage v-09 and at the same time leaves the medium ships almost out of the Surat lift. Couldn't we just retire v-09 from the rotation and put the medium hulls to work on Surat — at least 300 of Surat's men on medium ships? Walk me through what's pulling the optimizer the other way on both counts.
expected_pyomo:
model.ban_v09 = Constraint(expr=model.z['v-09', 'large'] <= 0)
model.surat_med = Constraint(expr=sum(model.y[vv, kk, qq] for (vv, kk, qq) in model.vkp if qq == 'surat' and kk == 'medium') >= 300)
